question.text: A company that has never been listed on any stock exchange wants to raise equity capital from the public. What type of offering will this be classified as?

explanation.text: **The Logic:** An IPO (Initial Public Offering) is the process by which a company offers its securities to the public for the first time and gets listed on a stock exchange. Since the company has never been listed, this is by definition an IPO. **The Trap:** An FPO (Further Public Offer) sounds similar but applies only to companies that are already listed and want to raise additional capital from the public. A Rights Issue is offered to existing shareholders, not the general public for the first time. **Cross-Exam Context:** The distinction between IPO and FPO is tested in NISM Series V-A (Mutual Fund) and Series IX (Merchant Banking) exams. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** IPO = First time listing. FPO = Already listed, raising more. This two-word distinction can save you marks on the exam.
